content.text: Prompt templates configure Claude's behaviour, safety posture, and cost profile for every feature that uses them. Without a structured review process, security gaps are not discovered until they are exploited, regression-inducing changes reach production without a baseline comparison, and no one can audit who approved a prompt change that caused an incident. The review process defined here applies to all new prompt templates and any change that bumps the minor or major version number.
A prompt change that passes automated tests but skips human review is not reviewed. Automated checks catch structural errors; human review catches intent errors that are syntactically valid.

attrs.alt: Prompt Review and Approval Swimlane Diagram

content.text: Writes the template, adds changelog entry, opens the PR, responds to review comments
Reviews for quality: clear constraints, correct structure, no redundant tokens, changelog complete
Required for customer-facing and tool-calling prompts. Reviews injection resistance, PII handling, capability boundary correctness
Yes — must approve before merge on in-scope prompts
Runs schema validation, injection pre-flight, eval suite, cost estimate. Blocks merge on failure
Promotes from staging to production canary, monitors eval metrics, completes full rollout or triggers rollback
Required if customer-facing or uses tools
Output format change; variable removed; identity section rewritten
New constraint added; output format refined; new variable with default
Required (can self-merge if prompt lead is the author)

A prompt is customer-facing if its output is visible to end users outside the organisation, or if user-supplied text is injected into the prompt at runtime. This covers all product-facing chatbots, document processors, classification endpoints called from the product API, and any endpoint where the requester can observe Claude's output.

Internal tooling (back-office automation, data pipeline prompts, developer-facing endpoints) is not customer-facing and requires a prompt lead review only, unless it handles personally identifiable information.
Add a prompt-specific PR template to the repository. It is automatically populated when the PR touches any file under prompts/:

<!-- .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/prompt-change.md -->
## Prompt Change

### Template(s) modified
<!-- List the template IDs and version bump (e.g. invoice-assistant: 1.2.1 → 1.3.0) -->

### Change summary
<!-- One paragraph: what changed and why. Link to the issue or experiment that motivated this change. -->

### Changelog entry
<!-- Paste the changelog entry you added to the template YAML here -->

### Behaviour change
- [ ] This change alters Claude's output format
- [ ] This change alters Claude's capability scope (adds or removes what it can/cannot do)
- [ ] This change alters the safety baseline
- [ ] This is a structural/non-behavioural change (typo, whitespace, comment)

### Testing
- [ ] CI schema validation passes
- [ ] CI injection pre-flight passes
- [ ] Eval suite pass rate ≥ minPassRate defined in template YAML
- [ ] I have manually tested the key scenarios against the new prompt
- [ ] I have checked the token count and it is within the budget for this integration

### Security review required?
- [ ] This prompt is customer-facing (output visible to end users)
- [ ] This prompt accepts tool calls or executes agentic actions
- [ ] This prompt injects user-supplied text into the system prompt section

If any box above is checked, tag @security-team for review.

Authors should open a Draft PR as soon as the template file exists, even if the prompt is incomplete. Draft PRs trigger CI checks without requesting reviewer time, giving early feedback on schema and injection tests. When the author marks the PR as ready for review, it enters the human review queue.
The GitHub Actions workflow runs four checks in parallel on every PR that touches a prompt file. All four must pass before the PR can be merged:
every required field present, version format correct, maxTokens within model limits, variables array populated.
send 8 known injection payloads through the template against a live Haiku instance; assert none alter the model's behaviour. This runs against the real API — not a mock.

run the golden dataset defined in evals/<template-id>/ and assert pass rate ≥ minPassRate from the template YAML. Blocked if the eval suite doesn't exist (new templates must ship with a baseline eval suite).

estimate the daily token cost of the change based on the median input token count of the golden dataset. Fail if the estimate exceeds 120% of the previous version's cost estimate (prevents accidental prompt bloat).

# .github/workflows/prompt-review.yml (excerpt)
name: Prompt Review Gates
on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['prompts/**']

jobs:
  schema:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install pyyaml jsonschema
      - run: python scripts/validate-prompt-schema.py

  injection:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: schema
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install anthropic pyyaml
      - run: python scripts/test-prompt-injection.py
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}

  eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: schema
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install anthropic pyyaml
      - run: python scripts/run-prompt-evals.py --changed-only
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}

  cost-estimate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: schema
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install anthropic pyyaml
      - run: python scripts/estimate-prompt-cost.py --changed-only --max-increase 1.2
The prompt lead is not running tests — CI does that. The lead reviews intent and quality:
Does the identity section accurately describe the product context?
A vague or inaccurate identity is the most common source of unexpected behaviour at the edges of scope.
Are the capability boundaries complete and unambiguous?
Review each DO NOT item: is it specific enough that Claude cannot rationalise around it? 'Do not discuss competitors' is specific; 'stay on topic' is not.
Is the safety baseline the current canonical version?
Check the version comment against the latest version in safety-baseline/.
Does the output format section match the UI's actual rendering requirements?
Review against the UI component that will display the output.
Is the changelog entry present and accurate?
It must describe the behaviour change, not the code change ('Added length constraint to prevent 800-word responses' not 'Updated system section').
Is the token count within budget?
Compare against the cost estimate in the CI output.
The security reviewer focuses on the attack surface, not prompt quality:
Review every variable interpolation site; confirm all user data is wrapped in namespaced delimiters in section 5.
Does the injection pre-flight result cover the endpoints this prompt will be used on?
If the endpoint accepts structured input (JSON, XML), test injections via those fields, not just the plain user message.
Does the capability boundary prevent disclosure of other users' data?
Specifically look for prompts that receive retrieved context — can a crafted query return another user's documents?
Are tool calls scoped to minimum required permissions?
A prompt that calls a database tool should specify exactly which tables or operations are in scope; 'read access to the database' is too broad.
Does the safety baseline cover the risk profile of this specific endpoint?
High-value financial endpoints may need extended baseline clauses not in the default template.

After merge to main, the CI pipeline deploys the new prompt version to the staging environment and runs a shadow eval: the staging endpoint receives a sample of real production traffic (with PII removed) and the eval pass rate is compared against the production baseline. If the shadow eval pass rate is below the threshold, the release is blocked automatically.

# scripts/shadow-eval.py
"""
Compare staging prompt against production baseline using sampled traffic.
Run after every merge to main before staging-to-production promotion.
"""
import asyncio, anthropic, json

async def compare_versions(
    staging_prompt: str,
    production_prompt: str,
    sample_inputs: list[dict],
    model: str,
    max_tokens: int,
) -> dict:
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
    staging_scores, production_scores = [], []

    for sample in sample_inputs:
        s_resp, p_resp = await asyncio.gather(
            client.messages.create(
                model=model, max_tokens=max_tokens,
                system=staging_prompt,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": sample["input"]}],
            ),
            client.messages.create(
                model=model, max_tokens=max_tokens,
                system=production_prompt,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": sample["input"]}],
            ),
        )
        s_text = "".join(b.text for b in s_resp.content if b.type == "text")
        p_text = "".join(b.text for b in p_resp.content if b.type == "text")

        s_score = await grade(s_text, sample["criteria"])
        p_score = await grade(p_text, sample["criteria"])
        staging_scores.append(s_score)
        production_scores.append(p_score)

    staging_rate = sum(staging_scores) / len(staging_scores)
    production_rate = sum(production_scores) / len(production_scores)

    return {
        "staging_pass_rate": staging_rate,
        "production_pass_rate": production_rate,
        "regression": production_rate - staging_rate,
        "acceptable": (production_rate - staging_rate) < 0.05,
    }

Never deploy a new prompt version to 100% of production traffic immediately. Use a canary rollout: send 5% of traffic to the new version for 30 minutes while comparing error rate and eval metrics. If the canary is healthy, ramp to 100%:
Error rate > 2× baseline OR eval pass rate drops > 3%
Monitor for 24 hours; rollback window remains open
When a prompt change is required to address an active security incident (e.g., a discovered injection vulnerability), use the expedited review path:
Open the PR and mark it with the label `security-expedited`.
The security reviewer (on-call rotation) has a 2-hour SLA to review and approve.
The prompt lead review is waived for security-only patches (changes touching only the safety baseline section).
All CI gates still run — expedited review does not bypass automation.
Deploy directly to 100% production without a canary rollout, with a 30-minute observation window before closing the incident.
Conduct a normal post-incident review within 5 business days.

Every state transition in the prompt lifecycle must be recorded in an immutable audit log. The log enables post-incident reconstruction ('what version was live when the incident occurred?') and satisfies SOC 2 change management controls:

content: Audit log entries are written to an append-only store (S3 with Object Lock, or CloudWatch Logs with retention policy). The

content.text: is the SHA-256 of the reviewer's GitHub username — never the raw username, to avoid direct PII storage.
Prompt templates that have been in production for 90+ days without a version update must undergo a mandatory quarterly review. The review checks that:
The pinned model ID is not deprecated or approaching end-of-life.
The safety baseline version matches the current canonical version.
The eval suite still covers the current scope of the integration (new product features may have expanded the input space).
The token count budget is still accurate (API pricing changes may affect cost projections).

The quarterly review does not require a version bump if no changes are needed — the reviewer adds a comment to the template file recording the review date and outcome, then commits it as a patch version bump.
Reviewer approves a well-written prompt that has a logic gap in the capability boundary
Use the structured checklist — read each DO NOT item and ask: can Claude rationalise around this?
An internal tool processes customer data; injection is possible via uploaded documents
Apply the customer-facing definition strictly: if user-supplied content is injected, it is in scope regardless of who the immediate caller is
Eval suite not updated with new scenarios
Pass rate looks fine but newly added product features are not covered
Require eval suite update in the PR for any minor or major version bump
A high-priority fix ships at 100% traffic and causes a regression visible to all users
Canary is not optional; the 30-minute canary period is the minimum, not a recommendation
Reviewer approves without a changelog; author adds it after as a push-force
CI blocks merge if the changelog is not present; reviewers should not approve without it
Version not bumped for a behaviour change
A wording change that alters model output ships as a patch version with no review requirement
CI compares behaviour on golden dataset; significant output change with a patch bump triggers a warning
Track these metrics to detect when the review process is becoming a bottleneck or losing rigour:
time from PR open to merge. Target < 3 business days for standard changes, < 2 hours for security-expedited changes. If cycle time > 5 days consistently, the prompt lead queue is the bottleneck.
which gate fails most often? High injection failure rate signals the team is not testing injection resistance during authoring. High schema failure rate signals the template format is unclear.
fraction of deployments that trigger a canary abort. Target < 5%. Higher rates suggest the staging eval is not catching regressions, or the eval suite is too lenient.
fraction of production deployments that require a rollback within 7 days. Target < 2%. Higher rates suggest the review process is missing something the production environment exposes.
fraction of templates with last-review date > 90 days that have a completed quarterly review on file. Target 100%.
Prompt reviews require two human approvals:
a prompt lead (quality and intent) and a security reviewer (attack surface, for customer-facing and tool-calling prompts).
schema validation, injection pre-flight (against the live API), eval suite pass rate, and cost estimate ceiling.
to capture what changed, why, the changelog entry, and which human reviews are required. This creates an audit trail for every prompt change.
to get CI feedback without requesting reviewer time. Convert to ready-for-review only when all gates pass and the changelog is written.
Run a shadow eval after merge and before staging promotion,
comparing the new version against the production baseline using sampled (anonymised) production traffic.
(5% → 25% → 100%) with eval metric monitoring at each stage. Abort if error rate exceeds 2× baseline or eval pass rate drops more than 3%.
For security patches, an expedited 2-hour review path exists
that waives the prompt lead review but preserves all CI gates and immediate full deployment with a 30-minute observation window.
